---
title: "Hyperverse"
subtitle: "Building the web from R, the way the web was designed."
author: "Arthur Bréant"
date: 2026-06-17
event: "Rencontres R 2026 in Nantes, France"
description: "Introduction to the hyperverse ecosystem: htmxr, plumber2, alpiner, glitchtipr. Presented in the « Production » session."
categories: [htmxr, plumber2, shiny, http, production]
image: img/intro_htmxr_hello.png
listing: false
format:
  revealjs:
    slide-number: false
include-after-body:
  - reset-videos.html
  - lang-switch.html
resources:
  - img/*.webm
  - reset-videos.html
  - lang-switch.html
lang: en
---

# Prologue

##  {.center}

::: {.r-fit-text}
**_"What you're doing with Shiny
is not real web."_**
:::

::: {.fragment .attribution}
My former boss
:::

::: notes
When I started R, before ThinkR, my boss told me one day:
'what you're doing with Shiny is not real web'.

I was offended. I was building apps, they ran in a browser, people used them:
what was his problem?
:::

## A few years later... {.center}

::: {.fragment}
He wasn't entirely wrong.
:::

::: {.fragment}
But not entirely right either.
:::

::: notes
Shiny is legitimate, it's what taught me to code interactive apps.

But Shiny doesn't do the web in the sense the web was designed for.
It's a desktop app running in a browser, with R as the engine.

Today I'm showing you another way — not better, different.
:::

## The trigger {.center}

::: {.fragment}
The _release_ of [`{plumber2}`](https://plumber2.posit.co/){target="_blank"} on CRAN.
:::

\

::: {.fragment}
&#x1F525; A modern HTTP server for R: routes, async, serialization, ...
:::

::: notes
The trigger was `plumber2` on CRAN in autumn 2025.

It's the package that lets you do the web "as we mean it" from R, with
standard HTTP requests and lots of modern features: async, middleware,
clean serialization.

Without plumber2, hyperverse doesn't hold up.
:::

##  {.center}

::: {.r-fit-text}
_Building the web from R,
the way the web was designed._
:::

::: notes
This presentation is the introduction to a young ecosystem: `hyperverse`.

A way that relies on web standards: HTTP, HTML, native browser components.

We'll see what changes — first on the mechanics side, then the arguments,
then the code.
:::

# hyperverse

## hyperverse {.center}

> _An R package ecosystem for building modern web applications,
> the web way._

\

::: {.fragment}
&#x1F525; **Not a Shiny replacement, just another approach.**
:::

::: notes
Official tagline of the site: a modular R ecosystem for building modern web
applications, the web way.

A framing I care about: we don't replace Shiny. Shiny remains legitimate
for analytics dashboards, prototypes, the specific ecosystem (DT, plotly,
bslib...). hyperverse answers different questions.
:::

## The stack at a glance

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
&#x1F4E6; **`htmxr`** &#x2192; **server** interactivity via HTTP

::: {.fragment}
&#x1F4E6; **`alpiner`** &#x2192; pure **client** interactivity
:::

::: {.fragment}
&#x1F4E6; **`htmxr.bootstrap`** &#x2192; styled components
:::

::: {.fragment}
&#x1F4E6; **`glitchtipr`** &#x2192; error monitoring
:::
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
\

_Today, focus on **`htmxr`**: the central piece._
:::
:::
:::

::: notes
Four packages, four distinct roles. We'll see them all again at the end of the talk.

But the main subject of the presentation is `htmxr` — the heart of
the ecosystem. The rest is complementary, and we'll touch on it briefly
at the end.
:::

## htmxr {.center}

> _The bridge between **htmx** (JS lib, HTML attributes)
> and **plumber2** (R HTTP server)._

\

::: {.fragment}
&#x1F525; You write **R**: `htmxr` generates **HTML** with the **htmx** attributes the browser can read.
:::

::: notes
htmx is a JavaScript library that adds interactivity via HTML attributes:
hx-get, hx-post, hx-target, hx-swap, hx-trigger. You never touch JavaScript
yourself.

plumber2 is the modern HTTP server for R mentioned earlier.

htmxr = the R layer that produces HTML with the right htmx attributes,
wired to plumber2 routes.

You write R, it works in the browser. Without writing a single line of JS.
:::

# The two mechanics

## How `Shiny` communicates

```{mermaid}
%%| echo: false
%%| fig-align: center
%%{init: {
  "theme": "base",
  "themeVariables": {
    "fontFamily": "Lato, system-ui, sans-serif",
    "background": "#f8f8f8",
    "primaryColor": "#1a1a1a",
    "primaryTextColor": "#f8f8f8",
    "primaryBorderColor": "#1a1a1a",
    "lineColor": "#2d2d2d",
    "actorBkg": "#1a1a1a",
    "actorBorder": "#1a1a1a",
    "actorTextColor": "#f8f8f8",
    "actorLineColor": "#2d2d2d",
    "signalColor": "#2d2d2d",
    "signalTextColor": "#2d2d2d",
    "noteBkgColor": "#ffffff",
    "noteTextColor": "#2d2d2d",
    "noteBorderColor": "#4a6cf7",
    "sequenceNumberColor": "#f8f8f8"
  }
}}%%
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant N as Browser
    participant S as R Server (Shiny)
    Note over N,S: Persistent WebSocket
    Note over S: Dedicated R session
    N->>+S: input$bins = X
    Note right of S: invalidates dependencies<br/>recomputes what's needed
    S-->>-N: output$plot = <PNG>
    Note over N: Shiny updates the element<br/>bound to output$plot
```

::: {.fragment}
&#x1F525; The server **keeps the pipe open**.
:::

::: notes
Shiny opens a WebSocket connection on load and keeps it open.
Each connected user = a dedicated R process on the server.

When a user moves an input, Shiny sends the value via the WebSocket.
The reactive graph identifies what needs recomputing. R recomputes, encodes
the result (often a base64 PNG), sends back via WS. Shiny.js updates
the DOM.

It's a "push" model: the server orchestrates, decides, sends.
:::

## How `htmxr` communicates

```{mermaid}
%%| echo: false
%%| fig-align: center
%%{init: {
  "theme": "base",
  "themeVariables": {
    "fontFamily": "Lato, system-ui, sans-serif",
    "background": "#f8f8f8",
    "primaryColor": "#1a1a1a",
    "primaryTextColor": "#f8f8f8",
    "primaryBorderColor": "#1a1a1a",
    "lineColor": "#2d2d2d",
    "actorBkg": "#1a1a1a",
    "actorBorder": "#1a1a1a",
    "actorTextColor": "#f8f8f8",
    "actorLineColor": "#2d2d2d",
    "signalColor": "#2d2d2d",
    "signalTextColor": "#2d2d2d",
    "noteBkgColor": "#ffffff",
    "noteTextColor": "#2d2d2d",
    "noteBorderColor": "#4a6cf7",
    "sequenceNumberColor": "#f8f8f8"
  }
}}%%
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant N as Browser
    participant S as R Server (plumber2 + htmxr)
    N->>+S: GET /plot?bins=X
    Note right of S: runs the route<br/>generates HTML
    S-->>-N: <svg>...</svg>
    Note over N: htmx swaps the SVG into #35;plot
```

::: {.fragment}
&#x1F525; The browser **asks**, R **answers**. No pipe, no R session.
:::

::: notes
The browser makes a standard HTTP request, R answers with HTML, htmx inserts
the fragment into the page.

No persistent connection. No R process per user.
The server carries no state between two requests — the client drives.

It's a "pull" model: the browser takes the initiative.
:::

## One phrase to remember {.center}

### In `Shiny`, the server **reacts**,

### In `htmxr`, the browser **asks**.

::: notes
This is THE phrase to engrave. To repeat at the mic.

This inversion of initiative changes everything:
- who carries the complexity (server in Shiny, client in htmxr)
- how it scales (sessions vs requests)
- what you can do with it (internal apps vs public apps)
:::

# The demo

&#x1F517; [hello.demo.hyperverse.world](https://hello.demo.hyperverse.world/){target="_blank"}

::: notes
The classic hello app (good old Old Faithful) served by plumber2 + htmxr,
on my OVH VPS. That's what we'll look at during the demo.
:::

##  {.center}

<video src="img/rr2026-loading-compare.webm" autoplay loop muted playsinline style="width:100%"></video>

::: notes
Side-by-side video captured with Playwright: same VPS, same hardware,
synchronized start. htmxr on the left, Shiny (via Shinyproxy) on the right.
Let the loop run 5–8 seconds, don't comment during — let them see.
:::

## Pretty _fast_, right? &#x1F60F; {.center}

## The benchmark

::: {.columns .center}
::: {.column width="50%"}
`htmxr`

──────────────────────

&#x1F517; [hello.demo.hyperverse.world](https://hello.demo.hyperverse.world/){target="_blank"}

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
`Shiny` _via Shinyproxy_

─────────────────────

&#x1F517; [open-apps.breant.art/app/hello-shiny](https://open-apps.breant.art/app/hello-shiny){target="_blank"}


:::
:::

\

&#x1F525; **Same OVH VPS, same hardware**, to make the comparison fair.

::: {.fragment .smaller}
\

_For reference, the `Shiny` app also runs on Posit Connect:_
&#x1F517; [connect.thinkr.fr/hello-shiny](https://connect.thinkr.fr/hello-shiny/){target="_blank"} _(different infra, not benchmarked)._
:::

::: notes
We're not comparing a personal VPS to a managed Connect cluster — that would
be dishonest. The main comparison is Shinyproxy vs htmxr, two containers on the
same OVH VPS-1. Connect is mentioned for those wondering "and with a real
professional deployment, what would that look like?".
:::

## Initial load

::: {.smaller}
_Wait time before the plot is visible in the DOM._
:::

::: {.columns .center}
::: {.column width="50%"}
`htmxr`

──────────────────────

&#x1F525; **0.3s**
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
`Shiny` _via Shinyproxy_

─────────────────────

&#x1F525; **0.9s**
:::
:::

::: notes
Protocol:

1. Warmup: 1 page load before the measurement → browser cache primed
   (Bootstrap CDN, Shiny assets, etc.)
2. Measure: 5 runs, median (not mean — robust to outliers)

Raw results:

  Run    htmxr (ms)  Shiny (ms)
  1      296         1200
  2      294         891
  3      252         974
  4      234         934
  5      321         871
  Median 294         934

The main comparison is Shinyproxy (same VPS, same infra as htmxr).
The Connect link is there to show an alternative professional deployment,
but we don't bench it — the infra isn't comparable.
:::

## Slider interaction

::: {.smaller}
_What happens when you move the slider, step by step._
:::

::: {.columns .center}
::: {.column width="50%"}
`htmxr`

──────────────────────

&#x1F525; _Near-instant_

- `GET /plot?bins=X`
- R generates an SVG in ~12 ms
- htmx swaps it in the DOM

&#x2192; Three steps, all standard.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
`Shiny` _via Shinyproxy_

─────────────────────

&#x1F525; _Micro delay_

- new value via WebSocket
- reactive graph invalidation
- recomputation
- PNG render, base64 encoding
- return via WS
- DOM update

&#x2192; Seven steps, several specific to `Shiny`.
:::
:::

::: notes
The Shiny micro-delay isn't "slow" in absolute terms — it's the direct
consequence of the path traveled.

My bench doesn't give me the Shiny number — it doesn't measure the WebSocket.
:::

## Client weight

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
![](img/minimeetup-dom-compare.png){.lightbox}
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
_Why these differences?_

&#x2192; **JS/CSS deps: 2 vs 13**

\

::: {.fragment}
&#x1F4E6; `htmxr`: 30 lines of HTML, **2 deps** (htmx.js + Bootstrap).

\

&#x1F4E6; `Shiny`: 48 lines, **13 deps** (jQuery, shiny.js, ionRangeSlider, bs3compat, ...).
:::
:::
:::

::: notes
The standard web philosophy: use what the browser already knows how to do.

Shiny made the opposite choice — rewrite components in JS for fine control
(ionRangeSlider replaces the native range input, bs3compat handles
Bootstrap 3 backward compatibility, etc.).

Both choices are defensible. But you can see visually why weight and
speed diverge.
:::

## _fast and cheap_, really? &#x1F914; {.center}

::: notes
Transition to the arguments. Speed is an effect, not the cause.

The cause is the architecture. And the most important effect for the
"Production" track is the bill.
:::

## #1 HTTP is **stateless** by design

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
`htmxr` (stateless HTTP)
──────────────────────

1 shared R process

RAM &#x267E;&#xFE0F; active requests

Idle user = **free**
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
`Shiny` (stateful WS)
─────────────────────

1 R session per user

RAM &#x267E;&#xFE0F; connected users

Idle user = **costs**
:::
:::

\

::: {.fragment}
&#x1F525; You pay for **actual work**, not idle sessions.
:::

::: notes
Quick vocabulary refresher before diving in:

- **Stateful** = the server keeps state between exchanges. It remembers
  who you are, what you clicked, the value of your inputs. In return,
  it allocates memory for each connected user, even idle. That's the
  Shiny model: a dedicated R process per session, that lives as long
  as the tab is open.

- **Stateless** = the server remembers nothing between two requests.
  Each HTTP request is self-contained, carrying everything needed
  to be processed (URL, query params, cookies, body). The server
  processes, responds, forgets. That's the HTTP model, so htmxr/plumber2:
  a pool of shared R workers, that respond on the fly without retaining anything.

Direct consequence on the slide:

- Stateful (Shiny): RAM grows with the number of **connected** users,
  even if they do nothing. An idle user = an R session occupying
  memory for nothing.

- Stateless (htmxr): RAM grows with the number of **in-flight** requests.
  An idle user = zero requests in flight = zero memory occupied.

A request in progress = memory. An idle user on their page = free.

Shiny does the opposite: every connected user pins an R session in memory,
even if they look at their phone for 10 minutes.
:::

## #2 The OVH bill

**At equal hardware** (VPS-1 OVH, €6.62 incl. VAT/month):

| | Architecture | Capacity |
|---|---|---|
| **`htmxr`** | 1 shared process | **1000+** users |
| **`Shiny`** _via Shinyproxy_ | 1 container / user | ~20 users |

\

::: {.fragment}
**At equal load** (500 active users):

| | Infrastructure | Cost |
|---|---|---|
| **`htmxr`** | VPS-1 OVH | **€6.62 incl. VAT/month** |
| **`Shiny`** _via Shinyproxy_ | dedicated RISE-1 64 GB | **~€78 excl. VAT/month** |

&#x1F525; **~12× on the bill, for the same user load.**
:::

::: notes
Two beats at the mic:

1. The raw observation on capacity — on the same hardware, the gap is ~50×.
   ~20 simultaneous Shiny users (1 container/user via Shinyproxy) vs 1000+
   htmxr users (1 shared process).

2. The fragment: at 500 active users, you go from a dedicated RISE-1 64 GB at €78 excl.
   VAT to a VPS-1 at €6 incl. VAT. ~12× on the bill, same user load.

Nuance: Shiny offers things htmxr doesn't (notably fine-grained reactivity).
But for an app with many users and few heavy computations, the balance tips
strongly toward htmxr.
:::

## #3 Interoperability: one single API

&#x1F517; [hello.demo.hyperverse.world/plot?bins=20](https://hello.demo.hyperverse.world/plot?bins=20){target="_blank"}

→ This URL returns **the SVG directly**.

Browser, `curl`, Python, another R app, webhook. Any HTTP client.

\

::: {.fragment}
The pattern: **same data, two serializers.**

```r
#* @get /api/rows                  #* @get /rows
#* @serializer json                #* @serializer html
function() diamond_data()          function() hx_table_rows(diamond_data())
#  ← curl, Python, JS              #  ← browser via htmx
```

&#x1F525; **Same backend, two clients.**
:::

\

::: {.fragment}
&#x274C; With `Shiny`, an app is only consumable **by a browser running Shiny**.
:::

::: notes
Three beats:

1. The live URL. You can paste it into anything that speaks HTTP — you
   receive the SVG, period. No app shell, no WebSocket, no R session.
   It's just a plumber2 route serialized as SVG. The most direct proof
   of interoperability.

2. The code fragment. You write your business logic once, you serve it in
   the format that fits each client. For data clients (curl, Python), you
   serialize to JSON; for the browser via htmx, you serialize to HTML (or SVG).
   Same backend, two clients.

3. The Shiny punchline. A Shiny app is only consumable by a browser running
   Shiny — you can't hit it with curl, you can't consume it from Python.
   It's a major strategic argument for production: your backend stays
   reusable, your data isn't prisoner of a single client.
:::

## #4 What you inherit for free

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
&#x2705; **HTTP cache**, **CDN**, replicas

&#x2705; **Horizontal scaling** (statelessness mandates it)

&#x2705; Standard **monitoring** (HTTP codes, nginx logs, metrics)
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
&#x2705; **Addressable URLs** (share via email)

&#x2705; Deployable **anywhere** (Docker, VPS, k8s, serverless)

&#x2705; **Debugging** via the browser DevTools
:::
:::

\

::: {.fragment}
&#x1F525;  **The web experience**.
:::

::: notes
When you do standard web, you inherit the infra everyone has already
figured out.

HTTP cache: nginx, varnish, cloudflare — you don't code anything.
CDN: you serve your assets from a CDN, R doesn't touch anything.
Addressable URLs: you can share /report?year=2025 by email, it works.

It's invisible but huge. With Shiny, you reinvent these building blocks
(or you do without).
:::

# On the code side

## A slider

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**`htmxr`**

```r
hx_slider_input(
  id      = "bins",
  label   = "Number of bins:",
  min     = 1,
  max     = 50,
  value   = 30,
  get     = "/plot",
  target  = "#plot",
  trigger = "input changed delay:300ms"
)
```
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**`Shiny`**

```r
sliderInput(
  inputId = "bins",
  label   = "Number of bins:",
  min     = 1,
  max     = 50,
  value   = 30
)
```
:::
:::

\


&#x1F4A1; Mental mapping preserved: same "standard" parameters + 3 attributes to wire up **the HTTP request**.

::: notes
htmxr components are designed to resemble Shiny's.

The difference: you EXPLICITLY declare what happens when the user
moves the slider — which URL to call, what to replace in the DOM, when
to trigger.

It's a bit more verbose, but it's also much more predictable:
no reactive magic, you see the HTTP request arrive in your logs.
:::

## The structure of an app

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**`htmxr`** (`api.R`)

```r
#* @get /
#* @serializer html
function() {
  hx_page(
    hx_slider_input("bins", ..., get = "/plot"),
    tags$div(id = "plot")
  )
}

#* @get /plot
#* @serializer none
function(query) {
  hist_svg(bins = as.integer(query$bins))
}
```
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**`Shiny`** (`app.R`)

```r
ui <- fluidPage(
  sliderInput("bins", ...),
  plotOutput("plot")
)

server <- function(input, output) {
  output$plot <- renderPlot({
    hist(x, breaks = input$bins)
  })
}

shinyApp(ui, server)
```
:::
:::

\

&#x1F4A1; Two route types: the full **page** and the HTML **fragments**.


::: notes
The structure of an htmxr app is different: it's an api.R file with
several plumber2 routes.

- The root GET / route returns the full page (hx_page + HTML structure)
- Each fragment route returns a chunk of HTML that will be inserted into the DOM

The #* @get and #* @serializer annotations come from plumber2.
:::

## What about `reactiveValues`?

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**`htmxr`**

```r
# /viz?cut=...

#* @get /viz
function(query, datastore) {
  datastore$session$cut <- query$cut
  render_viz(query$cut)
}
```

::: {.smaller}
&#x1F36A; `datastore$session`

&#x1F30D; `datastore$global`

:::
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**`Shiny`**

```r
r_global <- reactiveValues()

# r_global$cut <- ...

observeEvent(r_global$cut, {
  update_viz(r_global$cut)
})
```
:::
:::

\

&#x1F4A1; No reactive blackboard: each **concern** in its proper place.

::: notes
Reference for those who know: the `rv <- reactiveValues()` pattern
shared between modules, read via `observeEvent(rv$x, ...)`. ThinkR article:
https://rtask.thinkr.fr/fr/partager-des-donnees-entre-modules-shiny-un-update/

The problem: `reactiveValues` mixes three things in a single in-memory
object — the current UI, what the user did before, and computed results.
Everything lives in an R session, disappears when the tab closes, isn't
shared between users, doesn't survive a restart.

hyperverse separates these concerns, each in its native place on the web:

- **Current UI** (active filter, slider) in the URL: bookmarkable, shareable,
  back-button OK, indexable.
- **User preference** (which must survive between visits) in
  `datastore$session` (cookie + storr backend).
- **State shared between all users** (counter, leaderboard, cache) in
  `datastore$global`.

No reactive blackboard, no invalidation cascade, no module signature
that has to propagate `r`. All the details in state management:
hyperverse.world/state.
:::

## Not everything exists (yet)

::: {.fragment .fade-left}
&#128679; No [`golem`](https://thinkr-open.github.io/golem/){target="_blank"} / [`rhino`](https://appsilon.github.io/rhino/){target="_blank"} equivalent to structure a big app.
:::

::: {.fragment .fade-left}
&#128679; `htmxr.bootstrap` still in dev (unstable Sass theming).
:::

::: {.fragment .fade-left}
&#128679; `glitchtipr` / `alpiner` not yet published on CRAN.
:::

::: {.fragment .fade-left}
&#128679; No massive prod feedback yet: to be battle-tested.
:::

\

::: {.fragment .fade-in}
&#x1F525; **The ecosystem is young** &#x2192; open call for contributions.
:::

::: notes
Honesty: everything is not yet at the level of Shiny + golem.

To structure a big app, everyone finds their own convention today.
A golem/rhino equivalent for htmxr is yet to be imagined.

htmxr.bootstrap has bugs on Sass theming.
glitchtipr isn't published.

In short: it's young, it's open, and if the approach speaks to you, it's
the right time to contribute.
:::

## Who is `htmxr` for?

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**`htmxr` shines for** &#x2705;

- **Public** apps with many users
- Apps with **speed constraints** (even internally)
- **Tight budgets** (~12× cheaper on infra)
- **Reusable** endpoints: web, mobile, other services
- R devs comfortable with HTTP, or curious about the web
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**`Shiny` remains the right choice for** &#x2705;

- Internal dashboards, small audience
- Apps that depend on DT, plotly, bslib...
- Fine-grained reactivity needed
- Stack in place, tight deadline
:::
:::

\

::: {.fragment}
&#x1F525; Two tools, not a duel.
:::

::: notes
Positioning slide to deliver calmly, non-belligerent tone.

On the left: htmxr's playground. Wide public, production deployment,
interop. You want to be able to hit your endpoint with curl, from Python,
from your mobile app. You have more users than available CPU cores.

On the right: Shiny remains relevant. The 20 marketing folks who open
a dashboard once a day — perfect fit for Shiny. If you use DT/plotly
intensively, the ecosystem carries you. Fine-grained reactivity
(invalidation cascades), Shiny is hard to beat.

Punchline: "Two tools, not a duel." Important to close on this tone. You're
proposing an alternative, not a replacement.
:::

## hyperverse {.center}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
&#x1F4D6; **Docs** &#x2192; &#x1F517; [hyperverse.world](https://hyperverse.world){target="_blank"}

\

&#x1F680; **Live demo** &#x2192; &#x1F517; [hello.demo.hyperverse.world](https://hello.demo.hyperverse.world/){target="_blank"}

\

&#x1F4BB; **GitHub** &#x2192; &#x1F517; [github.com/hyperverse-r](https://github.com/hyperverse-r){target="_blank"}
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
<h1 style="margin: 0;">Thank you!</h1>
:::
:::
